of wisdom and the fear of God , that that be true wisdom . Job Xxviii . 28. and to man he say , behold , the fear of the lord that be wisdom , and to depart from evil be understanding . the great pre-eminence that man have over beast be his reason , and the great pre-eminence that one man have over another be wisdom ; though all man have ordinary the privilege of reason , yet all man have not the habit of wisdom . the great commendation that we can ordinary give a man be that he be a wise man , and the great reproach that can be to a man , and that which be worst resent be to be call or esteem a fool ; and yet as much as the reputation of wisdom be value and the reputation of folly be resent , the generality of mankind be in truth very fool and make it the great part of their business to be so , and many that pretend to see• after wisdom do either mistake the thing or mistake the way to attain ; commonl• those that be the great pretender ◊ wisdom and the search after it , place it i• some little narrow concern , but place i• not in its true latitude commensurate to th• nature of mankind : and hence it be tha• one esteem it the only wisdom to be ◊ wise politician or statesman , another t• be a wise and know naturalist , anothe• to be a wise acquirer of wealth , and th• like ; and all these be wisdom in their kind and the world perchance will be much better than it be , if these kind of wisdom be more in fashion than they be : bu• yet these be but partial wisdom , the wisdom that be most worth the seek and find be that which render a man a wise man this excellent man job after a diligen• search ( in the speech of this chapter ) after wisdom , what it be , where to be find do at length make these two conclusion ; viz. 1. that the true root of wisdom , and that therefore best know where it be to be find and how to be attain , be certain none other but a mighty God , vers . 23. God understand the way thereof and know the place thereof ; and 2. as he alone best know it , so he best know how to prescribe unto mankind the mean and method to attain it . to man he say , to fear God that be wisdom ; that be , it be the proper and adequate wisdom suitable to humane nature , and to the condition of mankind : and we need not doubt but it ( be ) so , because he , that best know what be the best rule of wisdom , prescribe it to man , his best of visible creature , who we have just reason to believe he will not deceive with a false or deficient rule of wisdom ; since as wisdom be the beauty and glory of man , so wisdom in man set forth the glory , and excellency , and goodness of God . and consonant to this David a wise king , and Solomon the wise of man , affirm the same truth , Psal. 111 10. the fear of the lord be the beginning of wisdom , a good understanding have they that do his commandment . Prov. 1.7 . the fear of the lord be the beginning of knowledge , but fool despise wisdom and instruction : and 9.10 . the fear of the lord be the beginning of wisdom , and the knowledge of the holy be understanding . and when the wise man have run all his long travel of experiment to attain that which may be that good for the child of man , in the end of his tedious chase and pursuit , he close up all with this very same conclusion , Eccles. 12.13 . let we hear the conclusion of the whole matter , fear God and keep his commandment , for this be the whole duty of man ; and he give a short , but effectual reason of it , for God shall bring every work into judgement , with every secret thing , whether it be good , or whether it be evil . and hence it be that this wise man , who have the great measure of wisdom of any mere man since the creation of Adam ; that have as great experience and knowledge of all thing and person ; that make it his business to search and to inquire , not only into wisdom , but into madness and folly ; that have the great opportunity of wealth and power to make the exact enquiry therein : this wise , and inquisitive , and experience man , in all his write , still the man fear God and obey he , the only wise man ; and the person that neglect this duty , the only fool and mad man . and yet it be strange to see how little this be think of or believe in the world : nay , for the most part he be think the wise man who have the least of this principle of wisdom appear in he ; that shake off the fear of God , or the sense of his presence , or the obedience to his will , and the discipline of conscience ; and by craft , or subtlety , or power , or oppression , or by whatsoever method may be most conducible , pursue his end of profit , or power , or pleasure , or what else his own vain thought and the mistake estimate of the generality of man render desirable in this world : and on the other side , he that govern himself , his life , his thought , word , action , end , and purpose , with the fear of almighty God , with sense and awe of his presence , accord to his word , that drive at a noble end than ordinary the world think of , namely , peace with almighty God , and with his own heart and conscience , the hope and expectation of eternity , such a man be count a shallow , empty , inconsiderate , foolish man , one that carry no stroke in the world , a man lade with a melancholy delusion , set a great rate upon a world he see not , and neglect the opportunity of the world he see . but upon a sound and true examination of this business , we shall find that the man that fear God be the wise man , and he that upon that account depart from evil be the man of great understanding . i shall show therefore these two thing ; 1. what it be to fear God ; 2. that this fear of God be most demonstrative the best wisdom of mankind , and make a man true and real a wise man . 1. touch the first of these , fear be a affection of the soul that be as much diversify as any one affection whatsoever ; which diversification of this affection arise from the diversification of those object by which this affection be move . i shall mention these four : 1. fear of despondency or desperation , which arise from the fear of some great and important danger that be unavoidable , or at least so apprehend ; and this be not the fear that be here commend to mankind . 2. fear of terror or affrightment , which be upon the sense of some great important danger , that though possible it may be avoid , yet it carry with it a great probability and immediate impendency , as the fear of mariner in a storm , or a fear that befall a man in some time or place of great confusion or visible calamity . and this kind of fear of almighty God be sometime effectual and useful to bring man to repentance after some great displeasure of almighty God by sin or apostasy ; but this be not that fear that be here , at least primary and principal , mean , but those two that follow . 3. a fear of reverence or awfulness ; and this fear be raise principal upon the sense of some object full of glory , majesty , greatness , though possible there be no cause of expect any hurt from the person or thing thus fear : thus a subject bear a reverential fear to his prince , from the sense of his majesty and grandeur ; and thus much more the majesty and greatness of almighty God excite reverence and awfulness , though there be no other ingredient into that fear . Jer. 5.21 . will you not fear i say the lord , will you not tremble at my presence , etc Jer. 10.7 . who will not fear thou , oh king of nation ! 4. a fear of caution or watchfulness . this be that which the wise man commend , Prov. 28.14 . bless be the man that fear always : and this fear of caution be a due care and vigilancy not to displease that person from who we enjoy or hope for good ; the fear of a benefactor , or of that person from who we may , upon some just cause or demerit , expect a evil , as the fear of a just and righteous judge . and these two latter kind of fear , namely the fear of reverence , and the fear of caution , be those that be the principal ingredient constitute this fear of God that these excellent man commend to we as true wisdom . now this fear of God arise from those right and true apprehension concern almighty God , that do with a kind of connaturality and suitableness excite both these two kind of fear ; and those seem to be principal these three : 1. a true and deep sense of the be of god , namely , that there be a most excellent and perfect be , which we call God , the only true God , the maker of all thing : but this be not enough to constitute this fear , for Epicurus and Lucian do believe that there be a God , yet be without the fear of he . 2. a true and deep sense , knowledge , and consideration of the attribute of God . and although all the attribute of God be but so many expression and declaration of his perfection and excellency , and therefore all contribute to advance and improve this fear , especial of reverence , yet there be some attribute , that seem in a more special manner to excite and raise this affection of fear , as well the fear of reverence , as that of caution and vigilancy ; as namely , 1. the majesty and glory of God , at which the very angel of heaven , that be confirm in a unchangeable estate of happiness , carry a inward , and express a outward reverence . 2. but majesty and glory without power be not perfect , therefore the sense and knowledge of the almighty power of God be a great object of our fear : he do whatsoever he please , all thing have their be from he , and have their dependence on he . 3. the deep knowledge of the goodness of God , and that goodness not only immanent in himself , but emanent and communicative : and from this diffusive and communicative goodness of God all thing have their actual be , and from he they do enjoy it . and both these goodness of God , the immanent and emanent goodness be the noble exciter of the noble fear , a fear spring from love , and that love fix upon the immanent goodness of God which be altogether lovely and perfect , and also upon the emanent and communicative goodness of God , as he be our benefactor ; and wherever there be this love , there be this fear both of reverence and caution . we can choose but honour and reverence , and be careful to observe and please whatsoever we thus love ; the intrinsic nature of that which we love for its own worth and perfection bind we by a kind of natural bond or result to reverence and honour , and the extrinsic emanation of that goodness unto we bind we to reverence and esteem , and honour it as our benefactor by a double bond , viz. first , of gratitude or benevolence to he that communicate this good ; second , by a bond of prudence and self-preservation not to disoblige he from who we have our good and upon who we have our dependence , lest a disobligation shall occasion his substraction or abatement of that good from we . wheresoever there be dependence , as there must be natural love to that upon which be our dependence , so there must be necessary a fear both of reverence and caution , even upon principle of self-love , if there be nothing else to command it . 4. a deep sense , knowledge and consideration of the divine omniscience . if there be all the other motive of fear imaginable , yet if this be want , the fear of God will be in a great measure abate ; for what avail reverence or caution , if he to who it be intend do not know it ? and what damage can be sustain by a neglect or omission of that fear , if God almighty now it not ? the want of this consi•eration have make even those atheist that •et acknowledge a god , such be Epi•rus , Diagoras , Lucretius , Lucian and other •mong the philosopher ; and such be Eli•has his oppressor , Job 22.13 . how do God now ? and can he judge through the thick cloud ? •r David be fool , Psal. 94.7 . the lord shall •ot see , neither shall the god of Jacob regard . but the all-knowing God search the very thought , and know the heart and •ll the action of our life ; not a word in •ur tongue but he hear it , and know our thought a far off . 5. a deep sense of the holiness and purity of God , which must needs cause in he a averseness unto and abhorrence of whatsoever be sinful or impure . last , the sense of the justice of God , not only a inherent justice which be the rectitude of his nature , but a transient or distributive justice , that will most certain distribute reward to obedience , observance , and the fear of his name , but punishment to the disobedient and those that have no fear of he before their eye . the deep consideration and sense of these attribute of the divine perfection must needs excite both the fear of reverence , and the fear of caution , or fear of offend either by commission of what may displease God , of omit of what be please to he . 3. but although this knowledge of almighty God and his attribute may just excite a fear both of reverence and caution , yet without the knowledge of some thing else that fear will be extravagant and disorderly , and sometime beget superstition or strange exorbitancy in this fear or in the expression of it , and a want regularity of duty or obedience ; if a ma• know that almighty God be just , and will reward obedience and punish disobedience yet if he know not what he will have do or omit , he will indeed fear to displease he , but he will not know how to please , or to obey he : therefore beside the former there must ( be ) a knowledge of the will or law of God in thing to be do or omit . this law of God have a double inscription , 1. in nature , and that be again twofold ; first , the natural rudiment of morality and piety write in the heart : second , such as be deducible by the exercise of natural reason and light ; for even from the notion of God there do result certain consequence of natural piety and religion , as that he be to be pray unto , to be praise , that he be to be imitate as far forth as be possible by we ; therefore as he be holy , beneficent , good , •erciful , so must we be . 2. but we have more excellent transcript of the divine will , namely the holy scripture , which therefore a man that fear God will study and observe and practise , as be the best •ule how to obey he . and the very fear of God arise upon the sense of his be and attribute will make that man very •ollicitous to know the will of God , and •ow he will be worship and serve , and what he will have to be do or not •o be do . and therefore since the glorious God have so far condescend , as by his providence to send we a transcript of his mind and will and law , he will be very thankful for it , very studious of it , much delight in it , very curious to observe it , because it be the rule and direction how he may obey and consequent please that great God who he fear ; this word he believe and prize as his great charter , and in this word he find much to excite and regulate and direct his fear of God : he see example of the divine justice against the offender of his law , of the divine bounty in reward the obedience to it ; threaten on one hand , promise on the other ; great manifestation of the divine goodness in the redemption of mankind by Christ Jesus , and therefore great ob••gations as well to fear as to love such benefactor . and thus far of the kind of the fear of God , and of the cause or object exciting it : now let we see how it do appear th• this fear man be the wise man , and how the fear of God discover it self to be the true and best and only wisdom ; which wi•• appear in these particular consideration follow . 1. many learned man consider that great similitude and image of ratiocination in some brute especial , have therefore decline to define a man by his reason , because of that analogical ratiocination which they find in brutes , but define a man by his religion , hominem est animal religiosum ; because in this they find no communication or similitude of nature or operation between man and brute beast : for man i• the only visible creature that express any inclination to religion or the sense of a deity , or any exercise of it . i do not stand to justify this opinion in all particular , only those thing be most certain ; 1. that only the humane nature seem to have any sense or impression of any regulary religion upon it : 2. that the sense of a deity and religion result from it , be the great ennoble , and advance , and perfection of the humane nature : 3. that take away the fear of God , all sense and use of religion fall to the ground . so that the fear of God be the great foundation of religion , and consequent the great ennoble and advance of humane nature , that seem almost as great a prelation of a man true religious above a irreligious man , as to operation and use , as there be between a irreligious man and a brute . as religion advance , so irreligion embase the humane nature . 2. justice be of two kind ; distributive , which be the justice of a magistrate or judge distribute reward and punishment , favour and displeasure , and due retribution to every man accord to the merit of his cause . 2. commutative , which be in all civil contract and deal between person ; as deal honest , keep promise , and use plainness , sincerity , and truth , in all a man say , or do : and both these kind of justice be effect of excellent wisdom ; without these state , and society , and person fall into disorder , confusion , and dissolution : and therefore those very man that have not this justice and righteousness , yet honour and value those that have it , and use it . and the fear of almighty God be that which beget and improve both these kind of justice . hence it be that Moses , in his choice of judge , direct that they shall be man fear God and hate covetousness . Jehosaphat , in his charge to his judge , think this the best expedient to contain they within the bound of justice , to put they in remembrance before who , and for who , they be to judge . and the very heathens themselves be some of they use to set a empty chair in the place of judicature , as a emblem of the presence of God , the invisible , and yet all-seeing God , as present in the court of justice , observe all the judge do ; and this they esteem a excellent mean to keep judge to their duty , by represent to they the glorious God behold they . and as thus in distributive justice , the fear of God be a great mean to keep and improve it ; so in commutative justice , the fear of God give a secret , and powerful law to a man to keep and observe it . and hence it be that Joseph can give no great assurance to his brethren , of his just deal with they then this , Gen. 42.18 . this do , for i fear God ; and on the other side Abraham can have no great cause of suspicion of ill and unjust deal from the people with who he converse , •han this , that they want the fear of God , Gen. 20.11 . because i think the fear of God be not in this place , etc the sense of the greatness ; and majesty , and power , and justice ; and all-seeing presence and command of almighty God lay a great obligation and engagement upon a heart fear God to deal just and honest , than all the terror of death it self can do . and if any one say , how come it to pass that the heathen that know not , and therefore fear not the true God , be yet great assertor , maintainer and practiser of all civil justice and righteousness between man and man ? i say , though they know not the true God , they know there be a God , who ( though ignorant ) they fear : and this imperfect and break fear of God be the true cause of that justice and righteousness that be sincere , and not for ostentation , practise among they ; and though they mistake the true God , yet in this they be not mistake that there be a god ; and this truth have that great prevalence upon they to do just : and if that imperfect fear of God in they do so much prevail as to make they so just , how much more must the true knowledge and the fear of the true God prevail to advance righteousness and justice in they that have that fear of God in their heart ? 3. it be a great part of wisdom that concern a person in the exercise of the duty of his relation , and indeed it be a great part of justice and righteousness . now th• fear of almighty God have these two great advantage therein . first , the will of God instruct exact all relation in their duty of these reciprocal relation ; and this will of God be reveal in his word , which contain excellent precept of all kind , suitable to every several relation . second , the fear of God set these direction close upon the heart , and be a severe and constant obligation to observe they : and so this fear of God do effectual fit , habituate , guide , and oblige a man to the duty of his several relation : it make a good magistrate , a good subject ; a good husband , a good wife ; a good father , a good child ; a good master , a good servant ; in all those several kind of goodness that be peculiar and proper to the several relation wherein a man stand . 4. sincerity , uprightness , integrity and honesty be certain true and real wisdom . let any man observe it while he will , a hypocrite , or dissembler , or double-hearted man , though he may shuffle it out for a while , yet at the long run , he be discover , and disappoint , and betray very much folly at the latter end ; when a plain , sincere , honest man hold it out to the very last ; so that the proverb be most true ; that honesty be the best policy . now the great privilege of the fear of God be , that it make the heart sincere and upright , and that will certain make word and action so : for he be under the sense of the inspection and animadversion of that God that search the heart , and therefore he dare not lie ; nor dissemble , not flatter , not prevaricate , because he know the pure , all-seeing , righteous God , that love truth and integrity , and hate lie and dissimulation , behold and see and observe he , and know his thought , word and action . it be true , that vainglory , and ostentation , and reputation , and design , and end may many time render the outward action specious and fair , when the heart run quite another way , and accord will frame the action , if those end and design and vainglory and ostentation be not in the way ; but the fear of God begin with the heart , and puri••fies and rectify it ; and from the heart thus rectify grow a conformity in the life , the word , the action . 5. the great occasion and reason of the folly of mankind be , 1. the unruliness and want of government of the sensual appetite on lust : hence grow intemperance and excess in eat and drink , unlawful and exorbitant lust ; and these exhaust the estate , waste and consume the health , embase and impoverish the mind , destroy the reputation , and render man unfit for industry and business . 2. the exorbitancy , and unruliness , and irregularity of the passion ; as excessive love of thing that be either not lovely , or not deserve so much love ; excess of anger , which oftentimes degenerate into malice and revenge ; excess of joy in light , trivial , inconsiderable matter ; excess of fear , where either no cause of fear , or not cause of so much fear be : and these exorbitance of passion betray the succour of reason , break out into very foolish , vain , imprudent action , and fill the world with much of that folly and disorder that be every where observable . 3. Theise disease and distemper of the mind , as pride , vainglory , ambition of honour and place , and power , insolency , arrogancy , envy , covetousness and the like , these i say be so many sickness and canker and rot ulcer in the mind ; and as they , like the fury that be let loose out of Pandora be box , do raise most of those storm and tempest that be abroad in the world , so they disease and disorder and beset the mind wherein they be , and make their life a torment to themselves , and put they upon very foolish , vain and frantic action and deportments , and render man perfect fool , mad man , and without understanding ; and their folly be so much the great and the more uncurable , that like some kind of frantic man they think very goodly of themselves , think themselves pass wise man , and applaud themselves ; though it be most apparent to any indifferent bystand , that there be not a sort of vain foolish person under heaven . now as we be true tell that the first degree and step of wisdom be to put off folly , — sapientia prima est , stultitia caruisse — so it be the method of the fear of God , the beginning of all true wisdom , to disburden a man of these original and foundation of folly : it give a law to the sensitive appetite , bring it in subjection , keep it within the limit and bound of reason , and of those instruction and direction that the wise God have prescribe ; it keep it under discipline and rule : it direct the passion to their proper object , and keep they within their due measure , and with in the due line and limit of moderation , and as become a man that live in the sight and observation of the god of glory , majesty , and holiness : it cure those disease and distemper of the mind by the presence of this great preservative and cathartick , the fear of God . if pride or vainglory begin to bud in the soul , he consider that the god he fear resist the proud : this fear put a man in remembrance of the glorious majesty of the most glorious God ; God be , both the physical , and ethical last end of all thing . 2. he give we all virtue , which be the way unto perfect blessedness . 3. he implant in we all good affection , which be the previous disposition unto virtue . 4. he give unto we the true and perfect blessedness . 5. nay , he himself be , our true and perfect blessedness . let we now proceed forward , from the politic , unto the ethic ; which afford we many argument , to prove there be a god : and that by the confession , even of the very heathen . for , as the physics do infer , that there needs must be a God ; because there be no more but one primum efficiens , Arist. l. 2.
